requirement: Product repositories keep their own agent memory and operating rules at the repository root.
Product repositories provide a short human entry point at the repository root.
Product source code lives under a stack-appropriate source tree when the selected product type needs one.
Product requirements live only under docs/product; root-level duplicate product documents are not part of the standard scaffold.
Product specification lives only under docs/product; root-level duplicate product documents are not part of the standard scaffold.
Product implementation plan lives only under docs/product; root-level duplicate product documents are not part of the standard scaffold.
Product workflow task state lives only under docs/workflow; root-level duplicate workflow files are not part of the standard scaffold.
Product handoff and restart context lives only under docs/workflow; root-level duplicate workflow files are not part of the standard scaffold.
Product memory and decision records use docs/memory as the standard directory while individual memory files remain optional until used.
Product developer memory lives under docs/memory when used; root-level duplicate memory files are not part of the standard scaffold.
Product session memory lives under docs/memory when used; root-level duplicate memory files are not part of the standard scaffold.
Product failure memory lives under docs/memory when used; root-level duplicate memory files are not part of the standard scaffold.
Product lifecycle stages are declared in a manifest rather than inferred from fixed stack names.
Product tests are declared in a manifest so stack-specific additions remain dashboard-readable.
Product CI expectations are declared in a manifest when CI is required or available.
Product integrations are declared in a manifest when external services are used.
Product security checks and approval needs are declared in a manifest when security gates apply.
Product dashboard-visible surfaces are declared in a manifest so the lesson dashboard reads product intent without stack-specific branches.
Product entrypoint, source, and test authorities are declared in a manifest so free-development repositories do not rely on root clutter or stack-specific inference.
Product display name and user-confirmed artifact identity are declared in a small profile so dashboards and workflows do not infer names from repository paths or prose.
GitHub Actions workflows remain in the standard root control directory and are declared by CI_MANIFEST.tsv.
Product-local Git hooks may exist when the product chooses them and are declared by ops manifests.
Product tests live in stack-appropriate test directories and are declared by TEST_PLAN_MANIFEST.tsv.
